author: @rutgerclarke2214
text: Yeah the tech has come a long way in recent years. Prusa do a hassle free pre-built printer (also user assembled if you want to save) and it's well designed, but it doesn't come cheap. But I do thoroughly recommend it for beginners who don't want headaches. I just acquired an independent dual extruder printer, you can print twice as fast, or printer with different filament types on the same model, which opens a whole new dimension to the printing and design process.

author: @stewartpalmer2456
text: Dr. Smith, I have both filament and resin printers. Both have their place. The resolution I get from my Elegoo Saturn resin printer is astounding. I absolutely love it. The filament printer was my learning machine. Great for prototyping. For slicing, "Cura for filament prints, Chitubox for resin." FreeCAD for model development. Blender for details.

author: @Aypaxable
text: I gave up on 3D printing being reliable AND affordable until I tried the Prusa i3 Mk3 series. They compete amazingly well in quality and accuracy to the $60,000+ printers I’ve used in the past which blew my mind. Looking forward to seeing how this Elegoo works out for you over time!

author: @TheEmbeddedHobbyist
text:
I have a Creality 3pro, ordered on a Friday arrived on the following Monday for less than £140. Only had to fix the gantry on and plug the wires in, oh and mount the reel holder. The bed adjustment was very close to spot on with only minor tweaks need. Unlike my Velleman Vertex K8400 printer which took a whole weekend to assemble and a week to get the movement correct. But i learnt a lot more about how it worked and how to fix issues than i did with the Creality.

And as another poster said getting a 3D printer is just the start, getting the best out of it is a whole new ball game. There is a lot to learn about model size on the screen and its size in the hand. Getting dimensional accuracy and clearance gaps is a steep learning curve but once stated the bends are fun.

author: @Twistedmetal-qe8kx
text: I've been using a prusa filament printer for years at work, its a production beast and worked out of the box.  The new generation of printers are in a new class.  Absolutely my goto tool, use it more than anything else.  You are spot on with the length of time the larger prints take, but I set it up at the end of the day and my part is waiting for me in the morning.  Great video, well worth the time to work up the learning curve.  The biggest thing is learning the 3d drawing cad program, after that you won't look back.

author: @PaulLemars01
text:
I've been on the 3D printing bandwagon since the Creality Ender 3 came out, so about 3 years I guess. We live in an apartment so no workshop/shed for us. I now have 2 FDM 3D printers in a corner of our living room and they are fairly constantly in use printing things that we need in our daily life. I'm currently working on my wife's lounger and turning it into something of a battlestation for her work (she works from home). All the fittings and mounts I've designed and printed in PETG since it's odorless and quite strong. I strongly recommend that you get used to a CAD package as quickly as possible so you can design your own stuff. I use Design Spark Mechanical since it's free and quite intuitive.

Oh and the printers are an Artillery Sidewinder X2 (very quiet and large print volume) and an Anycubic Linear Kossel because it's a delta type printer (looks like a spider spinning) and it's fast and looks cool when it's running.

author: @paulwright8378

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Some of the biggest downsides with 3d printing using filament include: 
1. poor print strength due to the fact that the layers never adhere as well to each other (As an analogy: think cold joints on concrete)
2. The 3D Model itself has to be of a certain design (no significant bridges, no overhangs, no floating bits etc)... unless the printer can print with some sort of structural filler that is easily removed... or the 3D printer head is a special multi-axis head design that allows for complex printing and rotation of the part... (very rare).
3. The typical materials used to print at decent cost are "structurally poor" and generally heat intolerant. So, things like PLA are just not good structurally for making long lasting parts. So, if you need high quality long lasting 3D parts, you will need to do research on using better more expensive filament.
4. When the filament breaks mid print --- its super hard to fix that unless the printer has filament break detection and a way to restart the printer mid-print.
5. They are very slow to print a part. 
6. (and a lot of other things like all the weird stuff that happens with filament printing)

But overall, to make toylike parts, prototypes, short lived parts... parts that don't really need to last, etc... usually works just fine. Youtube has some really great Content Creators for 3D printing to help anyone get started and learn amazing amounts without having to try to do everything yourself.

replies.author: @marcfruchtman9473
replies.text: At this point, I am strongly considering a large format resin printer instead, but the sizes I really need are stupid expensive.

author: @RaperJason
text:
Thanks Robert you made me feel really good 🤣 Ive been 3D printers for a few years now exactly the ones you described a box of bits and no instructions ANET were my chosen tools and I now have 4 of the things an 2 ANET one from aldi which is a very similar build to the one you have there and a freebie that someone couldn't get to work and they all print well most of the time
If you want a challenge try building a Delta mine was a bu**er to get working but once running they are fasinating to watch, reliable and the maths to make them work is awesome
Printed evrything from Hans Solos blasters a full size wearable storm trooper helmet and E11 blaster to my latest project a prototype for a ducted wind turbine
As for the wobble I mounted them in a 40mm box section steel cage so I can run them at very high speed and still get good prints
Be interested to see how you get on with the new one try printing ABS that is a true test for any 3D printer 👍

@ThinkingandTinkering I don't know if you have dabbled with fusion 360 it's a modeling program it's a full blown CAD Cam program it's got a steep learning curve it takes about three months and then all of a sudden you'll say damn I got it! At that point you don't really have it but you have it good enough to start being able to design some useful things and another year down the road you've designed some very very useful things I'm almost 60 years old and I learned it if I can learn it anyone can. author: @SimonPlatten
text: I'm on my 3rd 3D printer now....so far all three have had faults, the first which lasted the longest was the first QidiTech, it lasted 18 months before going faulty.  The next was a Crealirty CR10, this lasted almost a year then sent it back for a full refund, the next 3D printer is a Creality CR10SPro V2, this lasted 3 months before developing a very strange fault in the Z Axis.  I'm pressing for a full refund and keen to get something more reliable.

author: @jameshughes3014
text:
I've been playing with these for years. I love em. It's always good to have another tool.
Warning: unsolicited advice ahead for anyone new to this.
 printing FDM is a bit like any other shop tool. your first tries are gonna be terrible. maybe your whole first day. Just takes a bit to learn the process and quirks. But after that.. most of the time its smooth as butter. I can't count how many times I've realized 'oh.. i can just print that' and its been a huge bonus to have in my arsenal. Even if you're just using tinkercad with simple models. My best tip - Get a good brand of PLA filament. makes a WORLD of difference. Hatchbox and eryone brands have been good to me. Others make me wanna pull out my hair. If you're just printing functional parts.. wobble, temperature changes in the air.. all that really doesn't matter. Oh, second best tip - long thin parts should be printed horizontal, not vertical.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I thought exactly that mate and mostly I struggled with FDM - the Elegoo did print out right from the bat - nice tips by the way - cheers

author: @EgonSorensen
text:
I started out with a (too) small resin printer - Perfect prints, bit of a hassle.
Then I got the Neptune 3 from Elegoo, must say I kind of regret getting this - should have gotten a bigger resin printer. 
The quality 'just isn't there' - sure I can live with it, but having the choice I'd take resin over filament every time. 
Plenty of resins available, so lots to pick from to suit that specific need.  Found out that nylon like soaked in alcohol for 3-4 hours makes it quite soft and bendable - found out the box I made in PLA had a locking mechanism that quite easily snapped off. My 10c

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: I was sort of with you on that mate - but I can see the use and these are about 2-300 dollar mark - that's cheap - the problem has been so has the print - like the look but it is a first look and I will do a few more and see how it works out - but to be honest - good result on first try and with 3d it seems folks split into two camps - with this printer I am happy to have a foot in each camp - I have found issues with resin
